750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}

922	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
923	
924	\begin{theorem}\leanok
925	[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
926	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
927	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
928	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
929	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
930	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
931	  % NOTE (iter-009 review): the Lean encodes the augmentation in the
932	  % "augmentation-dropped" form `(K, e : A ≅ K.cycles 0, hexact : ∀ n, K.ExactAt (n+1))`,
933	  % equivalent to the blueprint's augmented complex `A → K•`; left-exactness of `G`
934	  % is `[PreservesFiniteLimits G]`. Verified faithful by the iter-009
935	  % lean-vs-blueprint-checker (PASS, 0 red flags).
936	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
937	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
938	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
939	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
940	  % L5785--5811).
941	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
942	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
943	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
944	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
945	  % $$
946	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
947	  % $$
948	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
949	  % $RF$."
950	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
951	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
952	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
953	  % and we obtain an exact functor
954	  % $$
955	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
956	  % $$
957	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
958	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
959	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
960	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let
964	  \[
965	    0 \longrightarrow A
966	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
967	      \longrightarrow \cdots
968	  \]
969	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
970	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
971	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
972	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
973	  \[
974	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
975	  \]
976	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
977	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
978	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
979	  object.
980	\end{theorem}
981	
982	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
983	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
984	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
985	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
986	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
987	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
988	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
989	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
990	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
991	% the long exact cohomology sequence
992	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}

13	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
14	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
15	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
16	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
17	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
18	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
19	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
20	
21	\section{Setup and motivation}
22	
23	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
24	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
25	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
26	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
27	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
28	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
29	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
30	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
31	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
32	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
33	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
34	
35	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
36	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
37	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
38	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
39	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
40	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
41	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
42	\[
43	  \begin{array}{ccc}
44	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
45	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
46	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
47	  \end{array}
48	\]
49	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
50	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
51	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
52	
53	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
54	
55	\begin{definition}
56	
57	\leanok
58	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
59	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
61	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
62	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
63	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
64	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
65	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
66	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
67	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
68	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
69	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
70	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
71	  % the unit ideal, see
72	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
73	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
74	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
75	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
76	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
77	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
78	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
79	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
80	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
81	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
82	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
83	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
84	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
85	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
86	  \[
87	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
88	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
89	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
90	  \]
91	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
92	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
93	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
94	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
95	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
96	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
97	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
98	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
99	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
100	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
101	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
102	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
103	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
104	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
105	\end{definition}
106	
107	\begin{definition}
108	
109	\leanok
110	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
111	  \label{def:cech_complex}
112	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
113	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}

115	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
116	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
118	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$

120	  % $$
121	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
124	  % \ldots
125	  % $$
126	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
127	  % \begin{equation}
128	  % \label{equation-extended}
129	  % 0 \to
130	  % M \to
131	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
132	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
134	  % \ldots
135	  % \end{equation}
136	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
137	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
138	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
139	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
140	  vanishing).}
141	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
142	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
143	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
144	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
145	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
146	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
147	  in degree \(p\) it is
148	  \[
149	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
150	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
151	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
152	  \]
153	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
154	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
155	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
156	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
157	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
158	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
159	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
160	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
161	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
162	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
163	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
164	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
165	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
166	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
167	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
168	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
169	\end{definition}
170	
171	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
172	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
173	
174	It is convenient to factor the construction of
175	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
176	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
177	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
178	
179	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
180	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
181	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
182	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
183	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
184	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
185	\[
186	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
187	    \;=\;
188	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
189	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
190	\]
191	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
192	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
193	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
194	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
195	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
196	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
197	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
198	
199	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
200	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
201	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
202	\[
203	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
204	  \qquad
205	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
206	\]
207	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
208	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
209	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
210	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
211	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
212	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
213	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
214	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
215	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
216	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
217	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
218	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
219	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
220	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
221	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
222	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
223	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
224	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
225	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
226	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
227	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
228	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
229	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
230	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
231	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
232	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
233	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
234	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
235	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
236	
237	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
238	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
239	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
240	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
241	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
242	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
243	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
244	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
245	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
246	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
247	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
248	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
249	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
250	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
251	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
252	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
253	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
254	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
255	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
256	
257	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
258	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
259	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
260	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
261	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
262	
263	\begin{definition}\leanok
264	[Arrow of an affine cover]
265	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
267	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
268	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
269	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
270	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
271	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
272	  apply.

307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}

311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[

373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of

504	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
508	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
510	  \mathlibok
511	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
512	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
513	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
514	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
515	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
516	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
517	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
518	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
519	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
520	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
521	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
522	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
523	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
524	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
526	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
527	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
528	\end{definition}

533	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
534	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
535	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
536	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
537	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
538	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
539	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
540	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
541	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
542	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
543	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
544	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
545	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
546	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.

630	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints

6097	  \(M_{g_\sigma}\). Since {\v C}ech cohomology transports along an isomorphism of coefficient
6098	  presheaves (Lemma~\ref{lem:cechCohomology_isZero_of_iso}), it suffices to prove the vanishing for
6099	  the tilde sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\): for every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\) and every \(p > 0\),
6100	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \widetilde{M}) = 0\).

6110	  unit ideal). Combining Steps 1 and 2 gives \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all
6111	  \(p > 0\) and every standard cover \(\mathcal{U}\); hence \(\mathcal{F}\) has vanishing higher
6112	  {\v C}ech cohomology for the affine cover system.

6115	\begin{lemma}[{\v C}ech cohomology transports along an isomorphism of coefficients]
6116	  \label{lem:cechCohomology_isZero_of_iso}
6117	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechCohomology_isZero_of_iso}
6118	  \uses{def:cech_cohomology_accessor, def:section_cech_functoriality}
6119	  \textit{Project-local transport lemma.}
6120	  Let \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) be an index family and let \(e : \mathcal{F} \cong
6121	  \mathcal{G}\) be an isomorphism of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If
6122	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for some \(p\), then
6123	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G}) = 0\).
6124	\end{lemma}
6125	\begin{proof}
6126	  \uses{def:section_cech_functoriality}
6127	  The section {\v C}ech complex is functorial in the coefficient presheaf
6128	  (Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_functoriality}), so \(e\) induces an isomorphism of section
6129	  {\v C}ech complexes \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong
6130	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G})\), and applying the degree-\(p\) homology
6131	  functor yields an isomorphism \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong
6132	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G})\). A group isomorphic to a zero object is itself zero.
6133	\end{proof}

6136	  iff its elements span the unit ideal]

6139	  \mathlibok

6141	  For a family \(s : \iota \to R\), one has \(\bigsqcup_i D(s_i) = \operatorname{Spec} R\) if and only
6142	  if \(\operatorname{span}(\{s_i : i \in \iota\}) = R\).

6145	\begin{lemma}[A standard cover of \(D(f)\) spans the unit ideal of \(R_f\)]
6146	  \label{lem:affine_cover_span_localizationAway}
6147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_cover_span_localizationAway}
6148	  \uses{lem:iSup_basicOpen_eq_top_iff_span}
6149	  \textit{Project-local infrastructure.}
6150	  Let \(R\) be a ring, \(f \in R\), and \(g : \iota \to R\) a finite family with \(D(f) =
6151	  \bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec} R\). Then the images
6152	  \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (g_i)\) span the unit ideal of \(R_f =
6153	  \operatorname{Localization.Away} f\):
6154	  \[
6155	    \operatorname{span}\bigl(\{\, g_i/1 : i \in \iota \,\}\bigr) = R_f.
6156	  \]
6157	\end{lemma}
6158	\begin{proof}
6159	  Pull the equality \(D(f) = \bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) back along the canonical open immersion
6160	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R_f \cong D(f) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\), whose underlying
6161	  continuous map is the comap of \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\). Comap commutes with
6162	  distinguished opens and with suprema, so the pullback of \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) is
6163	  \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i/1)\), while the pullback of \(D(f)\) is all of \(\operatorname{Spec} R_f\)
6164	  because \(f\) is a unit in \(R_f\). Thus \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i/1) = \operatorname{Spec} R_f\), which
6165	  by the span criterion (a family of distinguished opens covers the whole spectrum iff the elements
6166	  generate the unit ideal) is equivalent to \(\operatorname{span}\{g_i/1\} = R_f\).
6167	\end{proof}

6169	\begin{lemma}[Localisation transitivity for away modules]
6170	  \label{lem:away_comparison_isLocalizedModule}
6171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_isLocalizedModule}
6172	  \textit{Project-local infrastructure.}
6173	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M\) an \(R\)-module, and \(a, b \in R\). Write
6174	  \(M_a = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} a)\, M\) for the away
6175	  localisation of \(M\) at \(a\). Then the canonical transitivity comparison \(M_a \to M_{ab}\)
6176	  exhibits \(M_{ab}\) as the localisation of \(M_a\) at (the image of) \(b\):
6177	  \[
6178	    M_a\bigl[1/b\bigr] \;=\; M_{ab},
6179	  \]
6180	  i.e.\ the composite of the two away-localisation units is again the away localisation at the product
6181	  \(ab\). In particular, it yields the degreewise identification
6182	  \(M_{g_\sigma} \cong (M_f)_{g_\sigma}\) used in
6183	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover}: when \(f\) is invertible in \(M_{g_\sigma}\)
6184	  (because \(g_\sigma \in \sqrt{(f)}\)), localising \(M\) away from \(g_\sigma\) and localising
6185	  \(M_f\) away from \(g_\sigma\) present the same module.
6186	\end{lemma}
6187	
6188	\begin{lemma}[Two-step away localisation localises at the product factor]
6189	  \label{lem:isLocalizedModule_comp_away}
6190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.isLocalizedModule_comp_away}
6191	  \textit{Project-local infrastructure.}
6192	  Let \(R \to R_f\) be the away localisation of \(R\) at \(f\), let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module, and let
6193	  \(\mathrm{mk}_f : M \to M_f\) exhibit \(M_f\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(f\).
6194	  Suppose \(a \in R\) lies in the radical of \((f)\), say \(a^j = f \cdot h\) for some \(h \in R\) and
6195	  \(j \geq 1\) (equivalently \(f \mid a^j\)), and let \(g : M_f \to N\) be an \(R_f\)-linear map
6196	  exhibiting \(N\) as the localisation of \(M_f\) at the powers of the image
6197	  \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (a)\). Then the \(R\)-linear composite
6198	  \[
6199	    g \circ \mathrm{mk}_f \;:\; M \longrightarrow N
6200	  \]
6201	  exhibits \(N\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the powers of \(a\); that is, \(N = M\bigl[1/a\bigr]\).
6202	\end{lemma}
6203	\begin{proof}
6204	  Verify the three defining clauses of a localisation module directly. \emph{Units act invertibly:} a
6205	  power \(a^n\) acts on \(N\) through its image \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (a)^n\), which is
6206	  invertible because it is the localisation base for \(g\). \emph{Surjectivity:} any \(y \in N\) is
6207	  \(g(m_0)\) divided by a power of \(a\) for some \(m_0 \in M_f\), and \(m_0\) is \(\mathrm{mk}_f(m)\)
6208	  divided by a power \(f^l\) of \(f\); writing \(a^{jl} = (f h)^l = f^l h^l\) converts the \(f^l\) in the
6209	  denominator into a power of \(a\), so \(y\) is the image of \(h^l \cdot m\) up to a power of \(a\).
6210	  \emph{Cancellation:} if \(g(\mathrm{mk}_f(m)) = g(\mathrm{mk}_f(m'))\), chaining the cancellation
6211	  property of \(g\) and then of \(\mathrm{mk}_f\) produces a single power of \(a\) annihilating
6212	  \(m - m'\). This route avoids the converse of ``a localisation survives restriction
6213	  of scalars''.
6214	\end{proof}

6216	\begin{lemma}[Section {\v C}ech module exactness from a localisation-away spanning datum]
6217	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact_of_localizationAway}
6218	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact_of_localizationAway}
6219	  \uses{lem:section_cech_module_exact, lem:isLocalizedModule_comp_away}
6220	  \textit{Project-local infrastructure.}
6221	  Let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module, \(s : \iota \to R\) a finite family, and \(f \in R\). Assume
6222	  \begin{enumerate}
6223	  \item each \(s_i\) lies in the radical of \((f)\) (so \(f \mid s_i^{k_i}\) for some \(k_i\)), and
6224	  \item the images \(\{\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (s_i)\}\) span the unit ideal of
6225	    \(R_f = \operatorname{Localization.Away} f\).
6226	  \end{enumerate}
6227	  Then the un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet(s, M)\) of
6228	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} is exact in every positive degree --- \emph{without}
6229	  assuming that \(s\) spans the unit ideal of \(R\) itself.
6230	\end{lemma}
6231	\begin{proof}
6232	  \uses{lem:section_cech_module_exact, lem:isLocalizedModule_comp_away}
6233	  Run the computation over \(R_f\). Instantiate the polymorphic positive-degree exactness of
6234	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} over the base ring \(R_f\), with module
6235	  \(M_f = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} f)\, M\) and the family
6236	  \(s/1 : \iota \to R_f\); hypothesis (2) is exactly the spanning datum that lemma requires, so the
6237	  \(R_f\)-side complex \(D^\bullet(s/1, M_f)\) is exact in positive degrees. Transport this exactness
6238	  back to the \(R\)-side along the degreewise \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
6239	  \(M_{s_\sigma} \cong (M_f)_{s_\sigma}\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_comp_away} --- valid since
6240	  each \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma k}\) lies in the radical of \((f)\) by (1). These isomorphisms are
6241	  the vertical maps of an isomorphism of cochain complexes, commuting with the alternating-sum cofaces
6242	  by naturality of the localisation comparison, and exactness transfers across an isomorphism of
6243	  complexes.
6244	\end{proof}

6296	  \(f\)). \emph{(b) The cover spans over \(R_f\).} Because \(f\) is a unit in \(R_f\), the equality

6306	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_comp_away} (for a tuple \(\sigma\), \(g_\sigma = \prod_k g_{\sigma k}
6307	  \in \sqrt{(f)}\), so both sides present \(M\) localised away from \(g_\sigma f\)).

6311	  and coface matching used in Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} --- and wraps positive-degree
6312	  exactness as the vanishing of the degree-\(p\) homology, giving

6317	(Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) is proved by a dimension shift on the
6318	coefficient module, decomposed below into four sub-lemmas: the short exact sequence of
6319	{\v C}ech complexes coming from a basis (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}), the
6320	resulting stability of vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology under the injective
6321	quotient (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}), the sheaf-cohomology base case
6322	\(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}), and the
6323	dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)
6324	(Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}). The top lemma's proof then just
6325	chains these.

6327	\subsection{Section {\v C}ech infrastructure and the cover-system encoding}
6328	
6329	The sub-lemmas below are formalized in a \emph{cover-local}, presheaf-level, hypothesis-driven
6330	form: each lemma fixes a single index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) and works with the
6331	section {\v C}ech complexes of that one covering, taking the geometric inputs (section-level
6332	exactness on faces, positive-degree {\v C}ech vanishing) as hypotheses. The cover-global
6333	\((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) data of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} re-enters only
6334	in the inductive step (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), where it is packaged by
6335	the cover-system encoding (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system},
6336	Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) and discharges those hypotheses via the per-face
6337	short-exact-sequence derivation (Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}). The supporting
6338	definitions and the degreewise product engine are collected first.
6339	
6340	\begin{definition}
6341	
6342	\leanok
6343	[{\v C}ech cohomology accessor]
6344	  \label{def:cech_cohomology_accessor}
6345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechCohomology}

7133	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
7134	  % \begin{equation}
7135	  % \label{equation-extended}
7136	  % 0 \to
7137	  % M \to
7138	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
7139	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
7140	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

7142	  % \end{equation}
7143	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
7144	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.

7148	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7149	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
7150	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
7151	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
7152	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
7153	  \[
7154	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
7155	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
7156	    \qquad
7157	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
7158	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
7159	  \]
7160	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
7161	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
7162	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
7163	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
7164	  The object underlying this statement --- the augmented complex
7165	  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) of
7166	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmented_complex} together with its augmentation and the identity
7167	  \(\varepsilon \cdot d^0 = 0\) --- is already constructed; what remains is the
7168	  exactness (acyclicity) asserted here.
7169	\end{lemma}
7170	
7171	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
7172	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
7173	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
7174	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
7175	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
7176	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
7177	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. Note that
7178	% $M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$. ...
7179	% Let us define a homotopy ... by the rule
7180	% $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}$"
7181	% (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L80--110).
7182	\begin{proof}
7183	  \uses{def:cech_augmented_complex, lem:section_cech_homology_exact,
7184	    lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology,
7185	    lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma}
7186	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7187	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
7188	  We prove exactness of the augmented {\v C}ech complex of
7189	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_augmented_complex} in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) by showing that each homology
7190	  object \(\mathcal{H}^p\) (\(p \geq 1\), together with the augmentation node) is the zero object. The
7191	  argument works entirely with sections and sheafification, never with stalks.
7192	
7193	  \emph{Step 1: reflect through the forgetful functor to abelian sheaves.} The forgetful functor from
7194	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to sheaves of abelian groups is faithful and additive, hence
7195	  preserves zero morphisms; a faithful additive functor reflects the property of an object being zero.
7196	  It therefore suffices to prove that the image of each homology object \(\mathcal{H}^p\) under this
7197	  forgetful functor is zero in the category of abelian sheaves.
7198	
7199	  \emph{Step 2: the homology sheaf is the sheafification of the presheaf homology.} By the
7200	  sheafification-commutes-with-homology engine of
7201	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}, the degree-\(p\) homology of the complex of
7202	  abelian sheaves is the sheafification of the degree-\(p\) homology of the underlying complex of
7203	  \emph{presheaves}. The presheaf homology evaluated at an open \(V\) is the homology of the section
7204	  complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})(V)\) --- the {\v C}ech cochain complex
7205	  of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the cover restricted to \(V\) --- so the presheaf in question is
7206	  \(V \mapsto \check{H}^p(V, \mathcal{F})\).
7207	
7208	  \emph{Step 3: the presheaf homology vanishes locally on the affine basis.} This presheaf is not zero
7209	  as a presheaf, but it vanishes on a basis. Over any basic affine \(D(g)\) small enough to sit inside
7210	  a single cover element \(U_i\), the affine tilde isomorphism
7211	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma}) identifies \(\mathcal{F}|_{D(g)}\) with \(\widetilde{M}\),
7212	  and the section {\v C}ech complex of \(\widetilde{M}\) over the induced standard cover is exact in
7213	  positive degrees: this is precisely Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover} (whose
7214	  section-level exactness packaging is Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}). Since the basic
7215	  affines contained in the cover elements form a basis cofinal among all opens, the canonical map
7216	  \(0 \to (\text{presheaf homology})\) is a local isomorphism --- it is locally bijective. A map of
7217	  sheaves is an isomorphism if and only if it is locally bijective, and sheafification carries a locally
7218	  bijective map to an isomorphism; hence the sheafification of the presheaf homology is the zero sheaf.
7219	  Combined with Step 2 this gives \(\mathcal{H}^p = 0\) for all \(p \geq 1\).
7220	
7221	  \emph{Step 4: the augmentation node.} Separately, the augmentation \(\varepsilon : \mathcal{F} \to
7222	  \mathcal{C}^0\) is the kernel inclusion: the degree-\(0\) homology of the augmented complex vanishes
7223	  because over each basic affine the augmented section complex is exact at degree \(0\) (the same
7224	  spanning-family argument that gives exactness in positive degrees produces exactness at the
7225	  augmentation node), and sheafifying this local exactness as in Step 3 makes the augmentation node
7226	  homology zero. Together with Steps 1--3, every homology object of the augmented complex is zero, so
7227	  the complex is exact and the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} is a resolution of
7228	  \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
7229	
7230	  \emph{Remark.} An alternative proof uses the stalk-at-prime criterion directly: the complex is
7231	  exact if and only if it is exact after localizing at each prime \(\mathfrak{p}\), and for each
7232	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) one fixes an index \(i_{\text{fix}}\) with \(f_{i_{\text{fix}}} \notin \mathfrak{p}\)
7233	  and defines a contracting homotopy of the localized complex by
7234	  \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\), as in the Stacks source proof above.
7235	  This approach is equivalent to the sections-and-sheafification argument of Steps 1--4.
7236	\end{proof}

7238	\begin{lemma}
7239	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
7240	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
7241	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
7242	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
7243	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
7244	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
7245	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
7246	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
7247	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
7248	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
7249	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
7250	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
7251	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
7252	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
7253	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
7254	  \[
7255	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
7256	      \;\cong\;
7257	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
7258	  \]
7259	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
7260	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
7261	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
7262	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
7263	\end{lemma}
7264	
7265	\begin{proof}
7266	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
7267	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
7268	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
7269	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
7270	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
7271	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
7272	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
7273	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
7274	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
7275	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
7276	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
7277	  additive homology-preserving functor.
7278	\end{proof}

7289	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
7290	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
7291	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
7292	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
7293	  % associated to the presheaves
7294	  % $$

7300	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
7301	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
7302	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
7303	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
7304	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
7305	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
7306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
7307	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
7308	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
7309	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
7310	  \[
7311	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
7312	      \;\cong\;
7313	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
7314	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
7315	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},

7317	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
7318	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
7319	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream

7321	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
7322	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
7323	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
7324	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
7325	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
7326	
7327	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
7328	  of the preimage,
7329	  \[
7330	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
7331	      \;=\;
7332	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
7333	  \]
7334	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
7335	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the

7341	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
7342	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
7343	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
7344	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
7345	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
7346	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
7347	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
7348	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
7349	  \[
7350	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
7351	      \;\cong\;
7352	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto

7373	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
7374	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
7375	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
7376	  \to S\). Then:

7379	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
7380	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
7381	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
7382	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
7383	      \(k \geq 0\),
7384	      \[
7385	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
7386	      \]
7387	  \end{enumerate}

7392	% the sheaf

7398	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
7399	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine

7401	\begin{proof}

7404	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
7405	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
7406	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
7407	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
7408	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
7409	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
7410	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
7411	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
7412	
7413	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
7414	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
7415	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
7416	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
7417	
7418	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
7419	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
7420	  sheafification of
7421	  \[
7422	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
7423	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
7424	  \]
7425	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
7426	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
7427	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
7428	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
7429	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
7430	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
7431	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
7432	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
7433	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
7434	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
7435	
7436	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
7437	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
7438	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
7439	  for every \(k\),
7440	  \[
7441	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
7442	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
7443	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
7444	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
7445	  \]
7446	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
7447	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
7448	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
7449	\end{proof}
7450	
7451	\begin{lemma}
7452	[Each {\v C}ech term is $f_*$-acyclic]
7453	  \label{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}
7454	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic}
7455	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
7456	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
7457	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
7458	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
7459	  % L180--199).
7460	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
7461	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
7462	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
7463	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7464	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative affine vanishing).}
7465	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be separated and quasi-compact, \(\mathcal{F}\) a
7466	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(\mathfrak{U}\) a finite affine open
7467	  cover of \(X\) with all intersections affine. Then for every \(p \geq 0\) the
7468	  degree-\(p\) {\v C}ech term \(\mathcal{C}^p =
7469	  \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\)
7470	  is right \((\,\cdot\,)_*\)-acyclic for the pushforward \(f_*\):
7471	  \[
7472	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0
7473	      \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
7474	  \]
7475	\end{lemma}
7476	
7477	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
7478	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
7479	% the sheaf
7480	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
7481	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
7482	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
7483	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
7484	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
7485	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
7486	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
7487	% opens we win."
7488	\begin{proof}
7489	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, def:cech_nerve, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
7490	  A product of acyclic objects is acyclic, so it suffices to treat a single factor
7491	  \((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\), where \(s = (i_0, \ldots, i_p)\), \(U_s =
7492	  U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine, and \(j_s : U_s \hookrightarrow X\) is the open
7493	  immersion. Acyclicity for \(f_*\) is a local question on \(S\): the derived
7494	  pushforward \(R^k f_*\, \mathcal{G}\) is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
7495	  \(V \mapsto H^k(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\), so it vanishes for
7496	  \(k \geq 1\) iff that cohomology vanishes for all affine \(V \subseteq S\).
7497	  Restricting to such a \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the base change \(f_V :
7498	  f^{-1}(V) \to V\) is again separated and quasi-compact over the separated base
7499	  \(V\), hence \(f^{-1}(V)\) is separated and the affine \(U_s\) meets it in the
7500	  affine open \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V)\).
7501	
7502	  Now decompose the structure map \(g_s := f \circ j_s : U_s \to S\). First,
7503	  \(j_s\) is the inclusion of an affine open into the separated scheme \(X\), hence
7504	  an affine morphism, so the higher direct images \(R^q (j_s)_*\) vanish for
7505	  \(q \geq 1\). By Lemma~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} part (2),
7506	  \(R^k f_*\bigl((j_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\bigr) \cong
7507	  R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s})\). Second, working locally over an affine
7508	  \(V \subseteq S\) as above, \(g_s\) restricts to a morphism of affine schemes
7509	  \(U_s \cap f^{-1}(V) \to V\), which is an affine morphism; by the relative affine
7510	  vanishing \(R^k (g_s)_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_s}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\). That relative
7511	  vanishing is itself a consequence of Serre vanishing
7512	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}): \(R^k(g_s)_*\mathcal{G}\) is the
7513	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^k(g_s^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G})\), and for affine
7514	  \(V\) the preimage \(g_s^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which higher cohomology of a
7515	  quasi-coherent sheaf vanishes. Combining, \(R^k f_*(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for all
7516	  \(k \geq 1\).
7517	\end{proof}
7518	
7519	\begin{lemma}
7520	
7521	\leanok
7522	[{\v C}ech complex computes the higher direct images]
7523	  \label{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}
7524	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_computes_higherDirectImage}
7525	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
7526	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE; Lemma
7527	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent} ({\v C}ech computes cohomology when
7528	  % all intersections are affine) and Lemma
7529	  % \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}
7530	  % ($H^q(X,\mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^q f_*\mathcal{F})$ for affine $S$)
7531	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L245--256 and L843--854).
7532	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
7533	  % Let $\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering such that
7534	  % $U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ is affine open for all $p \ge 0$ and all
7535	  % $i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I$.
7536	  % In this case for any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ we have
7537	  % $$
7538	  % \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})
7539	  % $$
7540	  % as $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$-modules for all $p$."
7541	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
7542	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
7543	  % Assume $S$ is affine.
7544	  % For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$
7545	  % we have
7546	  % $$
7547	  % H^q(X, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S, R^qf_*\mathcal{F})
7548	  % $$
7549	  % for all $q \in \mathbf{Z}$."
7550	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Tag 02KE
7551	  (\texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}) and
7552	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
7553	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
7554	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
7555	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
7556	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
7557	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
7558	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
7559	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
7560	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
7561	  \[
7562	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
7563	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
7564	  \]
7565	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
7566	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
7567	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
7568	
7569	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
7570	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
7571	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
7572	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
7573	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
7574	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
7575	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
7576	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
7577	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
7578	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
7579	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
7580	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
7581	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
7582	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
7583	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
7584	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
7585	  role in the applications.
7586	\end{lemma}
7587	
7588	\begin{proof}
7589	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
7590	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
7591	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
7592	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
7593	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
7594	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
7595	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
7596	  \begin{enumerate}
7597	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
7598	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
7599	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
7600	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
7601	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
7602	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
7603	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
7604	  \end{enumerate}
7605	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
7606	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
7607	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
7608	  resolution,
7609	  \[
7610	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
7611	      \;\cong\;
7612	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
7613	  \]
7614	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
7615	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
7616	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
7617	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
7618	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
7619	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
7620	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
7621	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
7622	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
7623	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
7624	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
7625	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
7626	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
